content_markdown:
A first-harmonic standing wave is formed on a vertical string of length $3.0\text{ m}$ using a vibration generator. The boundary conditions for this string are that it is fixed at one boundary and free at the other boundary.

The generator vibrates at a frequency of $300\text{ Hz}$.

What is the speed of the wave on the string?

diagram_metadata.description: A diagram showing a vibration generator attached to a vertical string of length 3.0m. The generator is labeled with a frequency of 300 Hz. The string hangs downwards from the generator.

vibe_explanation: For a standing wave fixed at one end and free at the other, the first harmonic (fundamental frequency) occurs when the length of the string $L$ is equal to one-quarter of the wavelength, $\lambda/4$. Therefore, $\lambda = 4L = 4 \times 3.0\text{ m} = 12.0\text{ m}$. Using the wave equation $v = f\lambda$, we get $v = 300\text{ Hz} \times 12.0\text{ m} = 3600\text{ m s}^{-1}$, which is $3.6\text{ km s}^{-1}$.
